questions.stem: Inflation is best defined as what?
options: An increase in real output
A fall in the money supply
A sustained rise in the general price level
A one off rise in the price of a single good
questions.explanation: Inflation is a sustained increase in the general (average) level of prices, which reduces the purchasing power of money. A single price change or a one off jump is not, by itself, inflation.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The rate of inflation is most commonly measured using which indicator?
options: The unemployment rate
The level of the stock market
The consumer price index (CPI)
The exchange rate
questions.explanation: Inflation is usually measured by the percentage change in a consumer price index, which tracks the cost of a representative basket of goods and services bought by households.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A consumer price index uses a 'basket of goods' that is weighted. What do the weights represent?
options: The physical weight of each item
The age of each product
The number of shops selling each item
The relative importance of each item in typical household spending
questions.explanation: Each item in the basket is weighted by its share in average household expenditure, so that goods on which people spend more have a larger influence on the measured inflation rate.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Demand-pull inflation arises when what happens?
options: The costs of raw materials suddenly fall
Imports become much cheaper
Aggregate demand grows faster than the economy's capacity to produce
The labour force shrinks rapidly
questions.explanation: Demand-pull inflation occurs when total spending exceeds the economy's ability to supply at full employment. Excess demand pulls prices up. It is summarised as too much money chasing too few goods.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Cost-push inflation is caused mainly by what?
options: A rise in exports
An increase in consumer confidence
A cut in income tax
Rising costs of production, such as wages or raw materials
questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation comes from the supply side: higher wages, raw material prices, or import costs raise firms' costs, which they pass on as higher prices even without extra demand.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A sharp increase in world oil prices that raises firms' costs across the economy is an example of what?
options: Demand-pull inflation
Deflation
Disinflation
Cost-push inflation
questions.explanation: Higher oil prices raise production and transport costs throughout the economy, pushing the general price level up from the supply side. This is a classic source of cost-push inflation.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Who is most likely to LOSE from unexpectedly high inflation?
options: Lenders who are repaid in money worth less than expected
People holding assets whose prices rise with inflation
Borrowers with fixed interest loans
The government, when its tax revenue rises
questions.explanation: Unexpected inflation erodes the real value of money repaid, so lenders receive back less purchasing power than they expected, while borrowers gain. Holders of real assets are largely protected.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The term 'deflation' refers to what?
options: A fall in unemployment
A rise in the money supply
A slowdown in the rate of inflation
A sustained fall in the general price level
questions.explanation: Deflation is a sustained decrease in the general price level, that is a negative inflation rate. It is different from disinflation, which is a fall in the rate of inflation while prices still rise.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: 'Disinflation' describes which situation?
options: Prices are rising faster than before
Prices are falling overall
Prices are completely fixed by law
Prices are still rising, but at a slower rate than before
questions.explanation: Disinflation means the inflation rate is falling but remains positive, so prices keep rising, just more slowly. It should not be confused with deflation, where prices actually fall.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The labour force (working population for participation) is usually defined as which group?
options: Everyone living in the country
Those who are employed plus those who are unemployed and seeking work
Only people over the age of 65
Only those currently in work
questions.explanation: The labour force comprises people who are economically active: those in work plus those without a job who are available for and actively seeking work. It excludes the economically inactive.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If a country has a labour force of 20 million and 2 million are unemployed, the unemployment rate is:
options: 20 percent
10 percent
12 percent
2 percent
questions.explanation: The unemployment rate is the number unemployed divided by the labour force, times 100: $(2 / 20) \times 100 = 10$ percent.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Frictional unemployment refers to people who are what?
options: Temporarily between jobs as they search for new ones
Unemployed because of a downturn in the economy
Out of the labour force entirely
Out of work because their skills are no longer needed
questions.explanation: Frictional unemployment is the short term joblessness that occurs as workers move between jobs or enter the labour market. It exists even in a healthy economy because job search takes time.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Structural unemployment occurs mainly because of what?
options: Workers taking a short break between jobs
A general fall in aggregate demand
A mismatch between workers' skills or location and the jobs available
Seasonal changes in the weather
questions.explanation: Structural unemployment arises when the pattern of demand and production changes, leaving workers with the wrong skills or in the wrong place. Declining industries are a common cause.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Cyclical (demand-deficient) unemployment rises when what happens?
options: The economy goes into recession and aggregate demand falls
New technology replaces specific skills
Tourism falls in the off season
Workers change jobs voluntarily
questions.explanation: Cyclical unemployment moves with the business cycle. In a downturn, lower aggregate demand means firms produce and hire less, so this kind of unemployment increases. It falls again in a boom.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Ice cream sellers laid off in winter and farm workers idle after harvest are examples of which type of unemployment?
options: Seasonal unemployment
Cyclical unemployment
Structural unemployment
Frictional unemployment
questions.explanation: Seasonal unemployment is caused by regular, predictable changes in demand or production over the year, such as the end of the tourist season or the farming cycle.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The 'natural rate of unemployment' is best described as what?
options: The total unemployment during a deep recession
Only cyclical unemployment
Zero unemployment
The unemployment that remains when the labour market is in equilibrium, made up of frictional and structural unemployment
questions.explanation: The natural rate is the rate of unemployment consistent with the labour market clearing and stable inflation. It includes frictional and structural unemployment but not cyclical (demand-deficient) unemployment.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: 'Full employment' in macroeconomics is usually taken to mean what?
options: Literally every adult is in paid work
An unemployment rate of exactly zero
A situation where cyclical unemployment is zero, though some frictional and structural unemployment remains
Everyone works in the public sector
questions.explanation: Full employment does not mean zero unemployment. It means there is no demand-deficient unemployment, so the economy is at its natural rate, with only frictional and structural unemployment left.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: The original short run Phillips curve shows what relationship?
options: A relationship between inflation and the exchange rate
A positive relationship between inflation and unemployment
No relationship between inflation and unemployment
An inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment
questions.explanation: The short run Phillips curve suggests a trade-off: lower unemployment tends to come with higher inflation and vice versa. The curve slopes downward when inflation is plotted against unemployment.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: According to the long run Phillips curve associated with Friedman and Phelps, the curve is what shape?
options: Downward sloping at all times
Horizontal at zero inflation
Vertical at the natural rate of unemployment
Upward sloping at the natural rate
questions.explanation: In the long run, expectations adjust to actual inflation, so there is no permanent trade-off. The Phillips curve becomes vertical at the natural rate: inflation can be any level while unemployment returns to its natural rate.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Which of the following is a commonly cited cost of high and unstable inflation?
options: It guarantees lower unemployment forever
It has no effect on the value of savings
It always raises real output permanently
It creates uncertainty that can discourage investment
questions.explanation: Unpredictable inflation makes planning harder, distorts price signals, erodes savings, and can discourage long term investment. These costs are a main reason policymakers aim for low, stable inflation.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: 'Menu costs' of inflation refer to what?
options: The costs to firms of frequently changing and reprinting prices
The cost of food in restaurants
The cost of training new workers
The cost of importing goods
questions.explanation: Menu costs are the real resources firms use to update prices, catalogues, and labels when inflation forces frequent price changes. They are one of the microeconomic costs of inflation.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: A person who has given up looking for work because they believe no jobs are available is classed as what?
options: Part of the natural rate of inflation
Officially unemployed
Economically inactive (a discouraged worker), not officially unemployed
Employed part time
questions.explanation: To be counted as unemployed, a person must be actively seeking work. Discouraged workers who have stopped searching are classed as economically inactive, so official figures can understate joblessness.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: If the nominal interest rate is 8 percent and the inflation rate is 5 percent, the approximate real interest rate is:
options: 3 percent
13 percent
5 percent
8 percent
questions.explanation: By the Fisher approximation, the real interest rate is roughly the nominal rate minus the inflation rate: $8 - 5 = 3$ percent. This measures the return after allowing for the loss of purchasing power.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Hyperinflation is most commonly associated with which underlying cause?
options: Excessive growth in the money supply, often to finance government deficits
A short term fall in tourism
A small rise in import prices
A modest increase in wages
questions.explanation: Hyperinflation, where prices rise extremely fast, is typically driven by very rapid money supply growth, often when governments print money to cover large deficits. Confidence in the currency then collapses.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics

questions.stem: Government retraining schemes and improved job information are policies aimed mainly at reducing which type of unemployment?
options: Inflation
Cyclical unemployment
Structural and frictional unemployment
Seasonal unemployment caused by weather
questions.explanation: Retraining tackles skill mismatches (structural unemployment), while better job information speeds up matching (frictional unemployment). These supply side measures target the natural rate rather than demand-deficient unemployment.
questions.source: in the style of Sloman, Economics
